In client.py the suppress_warnings flag returns a warning `...Swap out url for https://api.plaid.com...` if not set to True upon client initialization. After reviewing the code it seems this flag must be set to True in order to use the development environment. However, I don't see it mentioned anywhere else in the code. 

Perhaps this flag could be named something more indicative of its purpose. I am not clear if I am actually suppressing warnings the api would have returned otherwise. Also, the url contained in the warning message is a bit unclear. I believe the urls follow an https://environment.plaid.com scheme, so I am not sure why it mentions swapping out the url for https://api.plaid.com.

I may be missing something so if anyone can provide clarification on this or point me to any documentation where it is mentioned I will close the issue. Thanks for all your work on the client.

issue_title: (Possibly) the anatomy of a music service plugin (collaborate on Spotify)
issue_body:
Hi everyone

There has been some request for information about how to write a music service plugin with all the bells and whistles (search, browse and integration with the data structures). I have been writing the first more or less complete one (Wimp) and tried to make the data structures for that purpose (MS*) general enough to make them work with other services as well. However, I should say, that I have only done 1 plugin, so I have been guessing what need to be generalized based on just 1 instance, so it is very likely that there are many mistakes and misconceptions. However, since Sonos does in many places seem to use existing standards, there is some hope that the assumptions I have made are actually correct.

Ideally I would write unit tests and docs for the MS* classes, and the second plugin before writing this up, but in the realization that it would take quite a while, here is the information I have, such as it is.

My second purpose with writing this post is to make a contact point for collaboration around a Spotify plugin. There seem to be several people interested in/working on this, so maybe you could get there faster by working on it together.

How to write a music service plugin
============================

The following is a point to description of what I think is required to a music service plugin.

Since the only implementation of this kind is Wimp, there will be quite a lot of references to that implementation. As of writing it is not merged yet, but can be read [here](https://github.com/KennethNielsen/SoCo/blob/wimp_part2_send_in/soco/plugins/wimp.py). It might actually be fruitful to start simply with a copy of the Wimp plugin and start from there.

1. Capture a Wireshark dump of a request to the music service server, to figure out the HTTP headers (set in module function `_get_header`) and the URL for the server (variable `self._url` set in `__init__`).

2. Capture a Wireshark dump of a request to the Sonos device for a session ID for the specific service. This probably has to be done when the client is started up or the first time in a client session that you browse information from the service. From the dump, get the service ID and put that in two places in the `__init__` method and in the module comment. Afterwards, check if the initialization works and gives you a session id. This is the first big assumption. I assume ... that the other services work in the same way, by getting a session ID from the Sonos unit.

3. In the dump from 1. Figure out how to create the description (in `desc` tags) and change the `description` property. It might involve attaching the user name to some string.

4. Try one of the search methods.

 4a. Intercept the return XML `result_dom` in the method `get_music_service_information` and print out the XML. Check that the results looks like a search result, hopefully with several result items and lots a data.

  4b. Check that the results are contained in either `mediaMetadata` or `mediaCollection` tags. Otherwise it will be necessary to modify the end of the method.

  4c. Check that the information tags for the results have tag names that are somewhat similar to the `valid_fields` list for the relevant MS* class (exception that "camelcase" names are turned into "underscore separated lower case" in the from_xml method). Don't be alarmed if you get exceptions from the data structures like: `The info tag \'...\' is not allowed for this item`. This just means that the there is a last one item that is has  not seen before, and it can just be added to the `valid_fields` list. This is the second HUGE assumption ... that the information elements are similar.

  4d. When using the `from_xml` method in the MS* classes, there are a few pieces of information necessary that is not in the XML. Therefore, this method also requires an instance of the music service class. It uses this to ask for the addition information (sort of reaching back). The fields that the MS* classes will require are: `id_to_extended_id` and `form_uri` methods and `description` and `service_id`. Until you actually want to try and add on the music elements to the queue, these can just be dummy functions.

5. Wireshark like crazy until you have a capture of all the types of playable content from a search being added to the queue.

  5a. From these captures figure out how to form the extended ID. The regular ID is contained in the result XML, but often an extended ID is used, where some extra information is added to the ID, possibly information about the type or the capabilities of the item. See the `id_to_extended_id` method and the `ID_PREFIX` module variable for inspiration.

  5b. Figure out how to form the URI. This may include the ID, the extended ID, an extension that has to be inferred from a MIME type or the service ID etc. See the `form_uri` property and the `URIS` module variable for inspiration.

  5c. The description property should be taken care of in point 3. Just check that it is identical in all the captures and fix up the `description` property accordingly.

6. At this point, hopefully all the information should be there to try and add one of the search results to the queue. If it does not work, get Wireshark dumps of adding the item with SoCo and the Sonos client to figure out the difference and hope that it is just a bug ;)

7. Most of the functionality should be in place for the browse function, except that you will need to repeat points 4a-4c and 5-5c while browsing around in the music tree, to get the data for the data structures that are only available through the browse function.

8. Finally, try to add one of the items to the queue and brace for impact.

Note 1) Not all of the data structures you encounter during browse are playable. This information should be in the XML and is available from the MS* objects via the property 'can_play'

Note 2) Not all of the data structures are used consistently. See the first note in the class docstring in Wimp.

This was a quick write up, as I'm quite busy at the moment, so fell free to correct pain-inducingly annoying spelling and grammar mistakes.

I hope this information will be of help and that I'm right about just half the stuff. I consider this issue a bug against the documentation, so it should be closed when we have experience from at least one more service and then the information in this issue (with corrections) can be put in the docs.

issue_title: Allow users to communicate to us their user deletion request
issue_body:
Since we will be storing user data on a central syncing server, we'll need a mechanism for a user to request their data to be deleted.

That can be performed by having another entry in their profile page give them an email template that they can send to `legal@learningequality.org`. The email should contain the following:

- user's UUID

We can then respond to their request in a timely manner.

issue_title: "Recent" filter might be too recent on coach reports
issue_body:
## Summary

Something I forgot to make an issue for during the last bug bash with Imps members. When they tried to view the coach report data that Khang and I made 1 week before that session on [http://192.237.248.135:8088/](http://192.237.248.135:8088/), they found they could no longer easily access it from Recent.

Navya only then observed that it is set at a hard "7 days". She noted that even though she might have her students work on content, she might still want to see performance on that content some time after 7 days. 

I remember that we decided to limit what shows by date in order to avoid overflowing the recent tab with content that goes on and on from the beginning of time, which I think is a good call.

issue_body:
During facility config, we allow users to hit 'save' even when no changes have been made. This results in the message "Facility settings updated!" popping up, which is not true.

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2367265/27186635-cd280cee-519d-11e7-986c-2675f28cb3cb.png)

We've tried both strategies when it comes to the question of "should a button be enabled even when it does nothing?". It's a subtle question that we should address in the "Forms Patterns" section of the style guide, but I think in this case there's a clear advantage to disabling the button when nothing in the form has changed.

issue_title: Producing reports that can be served successfully from Jenkins "userContent"?
issue_body:
Possibly just our own ignorance here, but...

We're finding the Scout2 report massively useful, especially for weeding out issues with our security groups.

However, it's so useful that we'd like to run it in a weekly scheduled Jenkins job and have the output generated into Jenkins [userContent](https://wiki.jenkins.io/display/JENKINS/User+Content) so the reports can be viewed easily.

The problem is that the generated report doesn't work when we do this - the same report files *will* work if downloaded locally and launched in the "normal" way.  General consensus seems to be that there's an issue precompiling the handlebars templates when it sits within Jenkins userContent.

Is there a relatively straighforward way to do what we're trying to do with Scout2, as it stands?

Thanks.

Steps:
1. add attorneys.py to the parse package
1. create attorneys def that accepts html
1. parse the attorney section
1. return a list of objects with attorney information

issue_title: Setting cache while using LoadFromStorage method
issue_body:
Hello,

I'm running into an error where Zeep is trying to cache something, but doesn't have permission to do so. I wanted to try either turning off caching or setting a directory where it could cache to confirm that this is the source of the error.

I'm using the LoadFromStorage method to create the dfp client, like so:

dfp_client = dfp.DfpClient.LoadFromStorage(filename)

I tried adding

cache: googleads.common.ZeepServiceProxy.NO_CACHE

to the .yaml file I am using in the LoadFromStorage() method

but when I start my server I get this warning:

/.virtualenvs/tmp/lib/python2.7/site-packages/googleads/dfp.py:203: UserWarning: Could not recognize the following keys: {'cache': 'googleads.common.ZeepServiceProxy.NO_CACHE'}. They were ignored.


Is there a way to indicate caching preferences when using LoadFromStorage? There's nothing in the documentation about that use case.

issue_title: charm build - not merging configs from lower layers
issue_body:
## Checklist

 - [X] Confirmed this is an issue with charm-tools, not charmstore-client
 - [X ] Provide versions of tools used
 - [ X] Described the feature or ways to replicate the issue

## What version am I running?

I ran the following command: `charm version` and got the following ouput:

```
charm 2.1.1-0ubuntu1
charm-tools 2.1.2
```

I am using: Ubuntu Xenial

## Issue/Feature

1. Config default values defined in my layer's [config.yaml](https://github.com/jamesbeedy/layer-gitlab/blob/master/config.yaml) don't seem to be making it into the built charm.
   
### I expect/expected the following
I expected to see my config default values defined in my layer's [config.yaml](https://github.com/jamesbeedy/layer-gitlab/blob/master/config.yaml) end up in my [built charm's config.yaml](http://paste.ubuntu.com/21659447/).

### What I got

The config definitions seem to make it into my built charm's config.yaml, just not the specified default values from [layer-gitlab config.yaml](https://github.com/jamesbeedy/layer-gitlab/blob/master/config.yaml). <- This is strikingly odd, because when I include another layer, say [juju-layer-node](https://github.com/battlemidget/juju-layer-node), following which build my top layer into a charm, I get the default configs for layer-apt, defined by layer-node in my built charm. This is different than what happens when I build my [charm-gitlab](https://github.com/jamesbeedy/charm-gitlab), which includes [layer-gitlab](https://github.com/jamesbeedy/layer-gitlab), which has the default values specified to satisfy the params for the layer-apt.

I hit one small issue while trying to **fontmake -o variable** over **.designspace** document.

It looks that **fontmake** will generate file names for master fonts while converting from .ufo to .ttf (calling **_font_name** method in **save_otfs**) while **varLib** expects .ttf files to have the same name as source .ufo.

No problem to change naming convention for UFOs (in designspace package), but I have a feeling that it should be improved, so varLib will get the file names from **save_otfs**.

2. Move it to the `fixtures` directory where necessary.

Getting rid of the `responses` directory should make things should make things straightforward for new contributors looking to add unit test coverage to their work.

issue_title: Separate unit tests from integration tests
issue_body:
Currently, there are many tests in the `test` directory that call external resources, rather than mocking requests/responses. Some also require environment variables (e.g. setting `EIA_KEY` in `test_eia.py`). This is causing issues in Travis and we're losing the value from existing continuous integration work that has been done.

I propose the following changes:
1. The codebase seems to follow a _[tests outside application code](https://docs.pytest.org/en/latest/goodpractices.html)_ directory structure, which is fine. In line with [this Stack Overflow thread](https://docs.pytest.org/en/latest/goodpractices.html) I'd further organize directories into:
```
pyiso
  + --- tests
  |       + --- unit
  |       |         | test_base.py
  |       |         | test_client_one.py
  |       |         | test_client_two.py
  |       |
  |       + --- integration 
  |                 | integration_test_client_one.py
  |                 | integration_test_client_two.py
  |       
  + --- pyiso
          | base.py
          | client_one.py
          | client_two.py
```
2. Fix `travis.yml` so that the build server only runs unit tests when pull requests are opened. I believe this is just changing the `[nosetests](https://nose.readthedocs.io/en/latest/usage.html)` script on line 26 to something like:
```
script: nosetests -w ./tests/unit
```

This only leaves one question: What automated process should run the integration tests? Should the maintainers just be responsible for running integration tests before formalizing a release? Should the integration tests just be there for developers to run out-of-band so they can be confident in their code before opening pull requests?

Deis now has support for Docker Swarm, Apache Mesos, and Google Kubernetes as application schedulers. With the known limitations of fleet (primarily, not being a resource-aware scheduler), we should investigate using a different scheduler as our default.

Is it possible to configure Deis to serve a specific app for the top level domain? I have a domain `example.com` under which apps go, `app.example.com`, and I want `example.com` to be forwarded to an app.

How do I go about doing that?
We need an etcd backup and restore procedure for a cluster. Right now, etcd could store only in ephemeral container FS, and a massive outage would wipe out all the data.

We should expand the documentation to cover the following topics.

#### The `Chain` class. 

* High level description of it's purpose/role/responsibilities
* Brief explanation of how it wraps VM classes.
* List of public methods (they don't need to be individually documented yet)

#### The `BaseChainDB` class

* High level overview of it's purpose/role/responsibilities
  * It's like an ORM for the `Chain` class.
* List of public methods (they don't need to be individually documented yet)


####  The `VM` class.  

* High level overview of it's purpose/role/responsibilities
* Basic explanation of the hierarchy of  `apply_transaction > execute_transaction > apply_message > apply_computation`.    
* List of methods and properties that can be set with simple descriptions of what each is used for


#### The EVM `Computation` API

* Enumerate all of the APIs that are accessible from the `Computation` object and the nested `GasMeter`, `Stack`, `Memory` , `Message`, and `CodeStream` apis.
* List of public methods and properties for these objects


#### Developers Guide

* Chains go in `evm.chains.<name>`
* Fork rules go in `evm.vm.forks.<name>`

* `py-solc` Version: 3.1.0

### What was wrong?

Calling `compile_source()` or `compile_files()` without including `ast` in the `output_values` parameter causes a `KeyError` here: https://github.com/ethereum/py-solc/blob/d0a9efad5a38c7b4143fb9c83b7dd0f5d1a88656/solc/main.py#L69

The reason is that `_parse_compiler_output()` unconditionally expects `sources` to be in the `solc` output json which isn't the case when `ast` isn't included.

issue_title: Cannot reinstall packages with conda - Anaconda 2
issue_body:
## Current Behavior I am using Anaconda2 and Python 2.7. I reinstalled it and I am adding packages through conda. In particular, I tried installing the R Kernel but it didn't work. Last week I did the same operation and it was working -- and the machine did not have changes in its configuration. I encounter the same problem with other packages. The error I get is an EncodingError. I tried running ```$ conda create -n py2 python=2 ``` and ```conda activate py2```but it did not help. I would like to keep on using Python 2.7 (because it is required for other reasons). Any help would be very much appreciated. ### Steps to Reproduce * Install Anaconda2 (https://repo.anaconda.com/archive/Anaconda2-5.2.0-Linux-x86_64.sh) * Ensure that I have ```/home/user/username/anaconda2/bin:``` in PATH * Execute ```conda install -c r r-irkernel ``` * I get as response: ``` EncodingError: A unicode encoding or decoding error has occurred. Python 2 is the interpreter under which conda is running in your base environment. Replacing your base environment with one having Python 3 may help resolve this issue. If you still have a need for Python 2 environments, consider using 'conda create' and 'conda activate'. For example: $ conda create -n py2 python=2 $ conda activate py2 Error details: UnicodeEncodeError('ascii', u'r-dbi\xa0', 5, 6, 'ordinal not in range(128)') ``` * I tried installing ```conda install -c r r-essentials ```and it worked, but then I get the same error with some other packages like ``` conda install -c r r-dbi ```, while ```conda install -c r r-rmariadb``` worked. ## Expected Behavior * The R kernel should be installed * A new notebook should be possible to be created using R ## Environment Information

issue_title: _'s in versions are not accepted by conda 4.3
issue_body:
This may need to be considered a blocker for 4.3 as I think it means that environments which were previously considered OK are now not OK.

When `conda skeleton cran` fetches r-packages from CRAN it replaces any `-` in the version with `_` (and helpfully puts a comment to that effect in the recipe), e.g.:

```
package:
  name: r-bit
  # Note that conda versions cannot contain -, so any -'s in the version have
  # been replaced with _'s.
  version: "1.1_12"
```

and also in the dependencies we also see such versions:

```
requirements:
  build:
    - r-base
    - r-bit >=1.1_12
```

Now, when conda is asked to install such dependencies I am getting:
```
failed to parse packages from exception: Value error: invalid package specification: r-bit=>=1.1_12
```

issue_title: Custom deactivate scripts in etc/conda/deactivate.d/ cannot modify the PATH environment variable
issue_body:
On Windows with conda 4.2.9, I am attempting to set environment-specific environment variables by following [this documentation](http://conda.pydata.org/docs/using/envs.html#windows).

I've set up the following custom activate/deactivate scripts in the environment folder.
In `etc/conda/activate.d/env_vars.bat`:
```set R_HOME=C:\Local\Programs\R-3.3.2
set R_USER=C:\Users\ENCPB\Documents\
set R_LIBS_USER=C:\Local\Programs\R_libs
set PATH=C:\Local\Programs\R-3.3.2\bin:%PATH%
```

In `etc/conda/deactivate.d/env_vars.bat`:
```set R_HOME=
set R_USER=
set R_LIBS_USER=
set PATH=%PATH:C:\Local\Programs\R-3.3.2\bin:=%
```

`R_HOME`, `R_USER`, `R_LIBS_USER` are properly set and unset, but not `PATH`. It is properly set but the deactivate script leaves `C:\Local\Programs\R-3.3.2\bin` in the PATH.

In the global Anaconda `Scripts\deactivate.bat` script, the `PATH` environment variable is actually set **after** the custom `etc/conda/deactivate.d/` scripts are run. Any change custom scripts do to `PATH` is ignored.

issue_title: conda install wrong version of jedi?
issue_body:
Hi all,

issue_title: ability to have conda reside in private environment
issue_body:
In this #3068, we add the ability for conda to reside in the `_conda` environment. Don't worry, conda will still work when installed in the root environment. This is achieved by having a Boolean config variable `config.conda_in_root` (and `config.conda_private` for convenience inience) in order preserve old behavior.  Also, because the `_conda` environment is considered private (because the environment name starts with `_`), it is not treated special in any way. That is, you can install or remove any package from `_conda`, even `conda remove -n _conda conda`.

To do (all these items imply that `config.conda_private` is `True`):
- [ ] `conda update conda` will have to somehow alias to to `conda update -n _conda conda`. Should regular usage of conda try self updates too?
- [ ] think about disallowing `conda install -n root conda`, as it would overwrite the symlinks.
- [ ] think about `conda install conda-build`. Right now I'm thinking that any package which lists conda as a dependency gets installed into `_conda`. Note that conda build works out of the box already when you `conda install -n _conda conda-build`. Think about implications for activate and deactivate.

The good news is that because of `config.conda_in_root`, we can for a long time (maybe even indefinitely) develop on a single conda codebase which supports both the classic root install of conda, and the new `_conda` environment.

What this means for the Anaconda (hopefully 4.2) installer, is that the anaconda and only its dependencies (which conda is already not) will be installed in the root environment, and conda in the `_conda` environment. The resulting installer will not be larger than before, and also not take up more space once installed, because of the use of hard-links. This will make Anaconda and Miniconda very similar, one has a populated root environment and the other does not.

When creating an environment like this:

`conda create -n testenv python=2.7 sphinx numpydoc`

Conda will install sphinx 1.3.1 and numpydoc 0.5 just fine.

However, the environment will have both sphinx 1.2.3 and sphinx 1.3.1 in the site-packages, and if you import sphinx you'll get 1.2.3.  Apparently the 1.2.3 version is included with numpydoc!

issue_title: openblas not built with full processor support
issue_body:
The openblas library appears to have been built only for the Nehalem processor. However, openblas has runtime cpu detection, and so it should be built with support for all processors.

Also, it's not clear which version of the library you are shipping - is it the sequential library, a pthreads library, or an OpenMP library? It would be good to have all three.

You can have a look at how it's supposed to be built at the Fedora spec file
 https://src.fedoraproject.org/cgit/rpms/openblas.git/tree/openblas.spec

For the dynamic architecture support, the flags you need are e.g.
TARGET=CORE2 DYNAMIC_ARCH=1

issue_title: Search for a gene by HGNC:ID does not work
issue_body:
When adding a gene to a gene panel (pending changes) searching for a gene by HGNC:ID does not work, only for the first two numbers then it tries to find aliases/synonyms etc. 

I also had one gene that had a too short name to be found, since the gene's name was included in many other gene names. Shouldn't a perfect match be found? (The gene was ICK, eventually I could find it by searching for synonyms to the gene - and found that it was already included in the gene panel...).

The websocket RFC doesn't require that websockets respect a redirect header, but I think it should. Shouldn't be relatively easy to implement. I'd support redirects by default, but allow redirecting to be disabled. Will also need to protect against infinite / recursive redirects.

issue_title: fixed position of  submit_line in change_form
[Bug] Compilation information in Issues tab misleading
issue_body:
After changing the compiler I've seen the following in Issues tab:

```
Compilation succeeded (output: mydir\bin\prog.dll)
```

But the Compiler tab says:

```
mycompilepath\cobc -o mydir\bin\prog.dll -std=default -debug prog.cob

cobc: prog.cob: No such file or directory
```

Therefore OCIDE either does not delete the outputfile [or rename if it renames it back when compilation failed] before calling the compiler and/or does not check if it's actually was created.

(The actual problem not finding the sourcefile is a different issue and likely on my side)

## System information


- OpenCobolIDE: 4.6.2
- Python: 3.4.3 (32bits)
- Qt: 5.4.1
- PyQt: 5.4.1
- pyqode.core: 2.7.0.dev2
- pyqode.cobol: 2.6.1
- pyqode.qt: 2.6.0
- pygments: 2.0.2

Especially for slower PCs/big source files/source files, when editing non-compilable files and everything is red, ... it would be nice to be able to disable/enable this function.

Enabling/disabling is currently done, but only internal if the COBOL compiler works/doesn't work. It would be nice to enable the user to do it via a toolbar switching button, too.

Currently azure-cli is at version 2.0.28 according to the [releases page](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/cli/azure/release-notes-azure-cli?view=azure-cli-latest) 

The latest version tagged in the [azure-cli-python repository](https://hub.docker.com/r/azuresdk/azure-cli-python/tags/) is version `:2.0.25` whereas the `:latest` has version 2.0.28 (`az --version`)

In our use case we have an image that extends the `azure-cli-python` image with a few scripts used in our CI.
We do not want to depend on a moving target like `:latest`, which can lead to inconsistencies between builds of the image. 
Currently there is a bug with the `az webapp deployment source config-zip` function in version 2.0.25 that makes it not work at all (#5440). This was fixed in a later version between `2.0.26` and `2.0.28`, but there is no version number tagged version that we can depend on after `2.0.25`.

It would be really excellent if new version releases were also tagged in docker again :-)

issue_title: az vmss get-instance-view returns model view when specifying all VMs
issue_body:
If you run the following to get the instance view for all VMs in a scale set:
```
az vmss get-instance-view -g rgname -n vmssname --instance-id \*
```
CLI does:
```
requests.packages.urllib3.connectionpool : https://management.azure.com:443 "GET /subscriptions/<subid>/resourceGroups/rgname/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachineScaleSets/vmssname/virtualMachines?api-version=2016-04-30-preview”
```
which returns the model view for all VMs.

It should be doing:
```
requests.packages.urllib3.connectionpool : https://management.azure.com:443 "GET /subscriptions/<subid>/resourceGroups/rgname/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachineScaleSets/vmssname/virtualMachines?$expand=instanceView&$select=instanceView&api-version=2016-04-30-preview”
```
The instance-view command works fine if you just query one VM.

issue_title: Compound collisions: Accessing the btCollisionShape pair for a contact point.
issue_body:
The `btManifoldPoint` class has the fields `m_index0` and `m_index1` that I thought represented the index of the `btCollisionShape` in the `btCompoundShape` that collided. Unfortunately, this won't work for recursive compound shapes and it doesn't appear to work for gImpact trimesh shapes either.

The `btGImpactCollisionAlgorithm::addContactPoint` function does the following:

```
m_resultOut->setShapeIdentifiersA(m_part0,m_triface0);
```

Which means that I get what I guess is the index of the trimesh face that collided, rather than the index of the gImpact shape in the `btCompoundShape`.

Can we add fields to `btManifoldPoint` that point to the pair of collision shapes?

There is some discussion related to this here: http://www.bulletphysics.org/Bullet/phpBB3/viewtopic.php?f=9&t=11056

## Long story short

Following the testing docs here: https://aiohttp.readthedocs.io/en/v0.22.3/testing.html

Specifically the `Unittest example`

I've recreated it (although without the loop part as I believe that's been deprecated).

Here:

``` python
import json
from aiohttp.test_utils import AioHTTPTestCase, unittest_run_loop
from aiohttp import web
from app import app

class MyAppTestCase(AioHTTPTestCase):
    def get_app(self):

    @unittest_run_loop

        request = await self.client.get("/healthz")
        assert request.status == 200
        text = await request.text()
        assert '{"status": "OK"}' == text

    @unittest_run_loop
```

issue_title: the example may need to change
issue_body:
This example 

```
await tracer.close()
import asyncio
import aiozipkin as az


async def run():
    # setup zipkin client
    zipkin_address = 'http://127.0.0.1:9411'
    endpoint = az.create_endpoint(
        'simple_service', ipv4='127.0.0.1', port=8080)

    # creates tracer object that traces all calls, if you want sample
    # only 50% just set sample_rate=0.5
    tracer = az.create(zipkin_address, endpoint, sample_rate=1.0)

    # create and setup new trace
    with tracer.new_trace(sampled=True) as span:
        span.name('root_span')
        span.tag('span_type', 'root')
        span.kind(az.CLIENT)
        span.annotate('SELECT * FROM')
        # imitate long SQL query
        await asyncio.sleep(0.1)
        span.annotate('start end sql')

        # create child span
        with tracer.new_child(span.context) as nested_span:
            nested_span.name('nested_span_1')
            nested_span.kind(az.CLIENT)
            nested_span.tag('span_type', 'inner1')
            nested_span.remote_endpoint('remote_service_1')
            await asyncio.sleep(0.01)

        # create other child span
        with tracer.new_child(span.context) as nested_span:
            nested_span.name('nested_span_2')
            nested_span.kind(az.CLIENT)
            nested_span.remote_endpoint('remote_service_2')
            nested_span.tag('span_type', 'inner2')
            await asyncio.sleep(0.01)

        await asyncio.sleep(30)
        await tracer.close()

if __name__ == '__main__':
    loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
    loop.run_until_complete(run())

```

The `await tracer.close()` may be need put outside the `with`. Otherwise the root span can't be sent to collecter.

issue_title: Export grades from the database through the formgrader
issue_body: There should be a button to download grades (via `nbgrader export`) through the formgrader.

It is basically a duplicate of the `ExecutePreprocessor` in IPython, it just does a few things that aren't currently in IPython proper. Once those things are in IPython, we can remove the `Execute` preprocessor in nbgrader.

See also:
* ipython/ipython#7883
* ipython/ipython#7884

Originally, it was not clear what the database would end up looking like, so mongodb made a lot of sense. However, seeing how the db tables/collections have evolved, I think it will really help to use sqlalchemy's ORM layer. This would remove the need for us to write and maintain our own custom ORM layer for MongoDB and would allow a much larger set of DB engines, most importantly file based ones that are easier to manage such as sqlite.

issue_title: pprinting inconsistent: sympy.subs() with noncommutative symbols
issue_body:
pprinting (init_printing()) gives inconsistent output when confronted when using sympy.subs() with noncommutative symbols:

Inconsistent behavior with pprinting:
`import sympy as sp`
`sp.init_printing()`
`a  = sp.symbols('a')`
`b  = sp.symbols('b',  commutative=False)`
`c  = sp.symbols('c')`
`ex = - a`
`ex.subs(a, b + c)`
'−c+b'
expected: '- (b + c)'

Expected and correct behavior without pprinting :
`import sympy as sp`
`a  = sp.symbols('a')`
`b  = sp.symbols('b',  commutative=False)`
`c  = sp.symbols('c')`
`ex = - a`
`ex.subs(a, b + c)`
`-(b + c)`

The issue does not arise when using commutative symbols. In similar fashion one gets more unexpected behavior (double minus sign):
'import sympy as sp'
'sp.init_printing()'
'a  = sp.symbols('a')'
'b  = sp.symbols('b',  commutative=False)'
'c  = sp.symbols('c')'
'ex = - a'
'ex.subs(a, -b - c)'
'−−c−b'

This again does not appear without the init_printing() statement. Again, the noncommutative part does not pick up the minus sign. However, If both b and c are noncommutative, one of them will still show the correct sign, be it accidental or not. In any case, the internal representation seems to be correct, as the issue disappears when turning of init_printing()

versions:
Jupyter notebook 4.1.0
Python 2.7.6
Sympy 0.7.6

@parleur fixed it in #825. 

We can do even better but the text in docs is irrelevant now.

When running pip install against version 4.2.0 I get the following error output:

    Running setup.py install for notebook ... error
    Complete output from command /home/ubuntu/miniconda2/bin/python -u -c "import setuptools, tokenize;__file__='/tmp/pip-0dtiHa-build/setup.py';exec(compile(getattr(tokenize, 'open', open)(__file__).read().replace('\r\n', '\n'), __file__, 'exec'))" install --record /tmp/pip-tgQif8-record/install-record.txt --single-version-externally-managed --compile:
    running install

The following required files are missing: ['notebook/static/components', 'notebook/static/notebook/js/main.min.js', 'notebook/static/tree/js/main.min.js', 'notebook/static/edit/js/main.min.js', 'notebook/static/terminal/js/main.min.js', 'notebook/static/auth/js/main.min.js', 'notebook/static/style/ipython.min.css', 'notebook/static/style/style.min.css']

issue_title: Opening Comm from kernel to unregistered front end target
issue_body:
This issue is about how the front end should handle the case when a Comm is opened from the back end (ipython kernel for example) to  a target that is unregistered.

Consider opening a Comm with the standard code from the wiki with an added `on_close` handler:

```python
from ipykernel.comm import Comm
my_comm = Comm(target_name='my_comm_target')
@my_comm
def _close(msg):
    print('Closed')
```

I would expect that if the `target_name` `my_comm_target` is not registered on the front end that my `_close` callback would be called to indicate that the peer is not connected but this is not the case. The Comm remains open unless closed from the kernel.

**Note**: The behavior described above is the way the ipython kernel currently handles unregistered targets when a front end comm is opened.

```javascript
comm = Jupyter.notebook.kernel.comm_manager.new_comm('my_comm_target');
comm.on_close((msg) => console.log(msg));
```

Looking at [services/kernels/comm.js#L84](https://github.com/jupyter/notebook/blob/7dc9439af78dadffcf6070c4b351d2d622f1d36e/notebook/static/services/kernels/comm.js#L84), he `Comm` should be created before looking up the target and if the lookup fails (the `load_class` promise is rejected) the comm should be closed. See [ipykernel/comm/manager.py#L75](https://github.com/ipython/ipykernel/blob/0a6898a153c66e2c2ade8cf3e0f199c102fbf33e/ipykernel/comm/manager.py#L75) for the back end implementation that I would like the front end to mirror.

I realize the README says this: "This project is still under development, so you should install it via GitHub instead of PyPI:" but IMO, `profiling` is definitely in a good enough position where a stable release can be thrown onto PyPI.

issue_title: lower_half() operation in logpdf (scipy.stats.multivariate_normal) results in asymmetric
issue_body:
I am aware that autograd reports that logpdf is not supported, but I saw the code as I had raised an issue with it few months ago. 
However, if I just use the code in autgrad/autograd/scipy/stats/multivariate_normal.py, the resulting jacobian for covgrad is asymmetric, and gets worse for tricky derivatives. 

Reproducible simple case
```python
test_cov = np.array(([[1.0, 0.5 ], [0.5, 1.0 ]]), dtype=float)
test_mean = np.array([0.0, 0.0], dtype=float)
data = test_mean + 0.1
A = np.random.randn(2,2)
A = A @ A.T

def dummy_test(test_cov, A=A, mean=test_mean, x=data):    
    scaled_test_cov = A @ test_cov @ A.T
    return logpdf(data, test_mean, scaled_test_cov)
jacobian(dummy_test)(test_cov)

```
```sh
array([[-0.64658309, -0.613179  ],
       [ 1.18318703, -0.55036653]])
```



**Fix is easy, remove lower_half and place 0.5 in line 43** 

I can understand this may slow down everything as we are duplicating the progenitors, but just passing the lower half gets incorrect results. 


#225 still happens as the override of primitive fails sometimes and once we are inside scipy lower level function all hell breaks lose. 
PS: If you wish I can raise a separate bug to track this issue. I think it has more to do with name wrangling in Anaconda than bug in autograd itself.

issue_title: planemo lint XSD check unaware of legacy parameter attribute default_value
issue_body:
While `<param value="..." ...>` is prefered, Galaxy still supports `<param default_value="..." ...>` for a `ColumnListParameter`, as can be seen here:

https://github.com/galaxyproject/galaxy/blob/dev/lib/galaxy/tools/parameters/basic.py#L1005

``` python
        # Legacy style default value specification...
        self.default_value = input_source.get( "default_value", None )
        if self.default_value is None:
            # Newer style... more in line with other parameters.
            self.default_value = input_source.get( "value", None )
        if self.default_value is not None:
            self.default_value = ColumnListParameter._strip_c( self.default_value )
```

The XSD should be aware of this legacy attribute, which currently causes lint failures, e.g. https://travis-ci.org/peterjc/galaxy_blast/jobs/167595623

Updated tags `latest` and `latest-tmp` still trigger AppVeyor build even if done in follow up commit (in that particular case https://github.com/scikit-build/scikit-ci-addons/commit/9a65d296059d34c4b7a858aca04b1447a0d53411):

issue_title: noop puppet runs with no changes reporting as 'noop' instead of 'unchanged' for puppetdb > 4.1.0
issue_body:
Scenario: puppet is running as --noop every 30 minutes, and so far they were being reported in puppetboard as 'unchanged'; which is exactly what I expected, as no 'noops' events existed. It was pypuppetdb 0.3.1, puppetdb 4.0.x. 


Something example code to reproduce it:
```
from pypuppetdb import connect

puppetdb = connect(host='puppetdb', port=8080, timeout=20,)
nodes = puppetdb.nodes(with_status=True)
for node in nodes:
  print(node.name + ' - ' + puppet.status)
```

Looking at https://github.com/voxpupuli/pypuppetdb/blob/master/pypuppetdb/api.py#L413, it appears that I was getting in the 'else' clause, because 'latest_report_noop' was only included in puppetdb [4.1.0](https://docs.puppet.com/puppetdb/latest/release_notes.html#section-6):

```
                    if node['latest_report_noop']:
                        node['status'] = 'noop'
                    else:
                        node['status'] = node['latest_report_status']
```


I upgraded puppetdb from 4.0.x to 4.2.x. And suddenly now those same nodes are reporting as 'noop' regardless if there are changes or not to be applied. I suppose you wouldn't expect such change of behaviour, and in all fairness, puppetboard does treat 'noop' as 'pending changes'. 

Shouldn't it be using `latest_report_noop_pending` instead of `latest_report_noop`?



In case it helps, an example output in puppetdb 4.2.x, after noop without changes.

issue_title: Incorrect file attributes for Debian 8 RC3 SDK binaries
issue_body:
For some reason the file attributes for the SDK .dlls & .exes shipped in the Debian 8 RC3 binary release are set to 600 which seems to only allow the file owner to execute dotnet commands. In 1.0.0-preview2-1-003177 these were set to 644. This is probably okay for a single user but not ideal for build servers (where I came across this issue).

A quick fix is to run `sudo find /opt/dotnet/sdk/1.0.0-rc3-004530/ -type f -name '*.dll' -exec chmod 644 {} \; ` & `sudo find /opt/dotnet/sdk/1.0.0-rc3-004530/ -type f -name '*.exe' -exec chmod 644 {} \; `

As this is a release/distribution issue I'm not sure which repository to log this issue in.

issue_title: post_dump: where are the raw data ?
URLFor and AbsoluteURLFor fail if URL attribute is None
issue_body:
This issue has been reported before in #18 but I wanted to provide a simple example showing how this is a problem for a lot of use cases:

```
class User(db.Model):
    id = db.Column(db.Integer, primary_key=True)
    group_id = Column(db.Integer, db.ForeignKey('groups.id'), default=None)

class UserSchema(ma.ModelSchema):
   class Meta:
      model = User
   group = ma.AbsoluteURLFor('groups', id='<group_id>')

class Group(db.Model):
   id = Column(Integer, primary_key=True)
```
As far as I know, there is no requirement for the user to have a `group_id`, so this use case makes sense.

Currently, the `AbsoluteURLFor` method will fail with a `werkzeug.routing.BuildError` if `group_id` is not set on the `User` model.

I know this problem originates directly from `Flask` `url_for` method, that would fail if `id` is `None`, but there should be a way in `Flask-Marshmallow` to skip serialization for this field when `None`.

issue_title: Relation types only take into account model names which are not always correct.
When using the ListCreateApiView, the action has to be differentiated
issue_body:
I had the problem that I couldn't use the generics.ListCreateAPIView for both, getting a list of all models and posting a new model.

If I set the action to 'list', I could correctly deliver a JsonAPI list of all the models; but when posting, the JSONRenderer had a problem on line 63 (`results = (data["results"] if isinstance(data, dict) else data)`) because there is no 'results' in the data dict. 

However, when I removed the `action = 'list'` from my view, the post was done correctly, but the list of models wasn't in JsonAPI.

I now set up my view like this:

    class AddressList(generics.ListCreateAPIView):
        """List of addresses"""
        serializer_class = models.AddressSerializer

        def get(self, request, *args, **kwargs):
            self.action = 'list'
            return super(AddressList, self).get(request, *args, **kwargs)

            [...]

which works, but I wonder, if this is the best way to go...

I will make a 0.5.1 bugfix release; in order to stay within the rules of semantic versioning I will rewrite the develop history to move the merged PR #66 *after* the release (because it contains new functionality).

My enviroment
Ubuntu 16.04.1 LTS (xenial)
Python 3.5.2 
Z3 version 4.5.1 - 64 (commit: 69aa5ca877f5de0a2c00515d0fe86a797b95701a)

The docs refer to `PROOF_MODE` having values of `1` or `2`.

However, this is the old name for the parameter and it is now `proof` and only boolean, as far as I can tell.

Should the docs be updated to reflect that the parameter is `PROOF` and the value is `true` and not `1`?

What about the case(s) where `PROOF_MODE` was `2`?

There is a bug in Z3.FloatingPoint.get_ebits and Z3.FloatingPoint.get_sbits I stumbled
upon during a debugging session. Please see following code:

```
let _ =
    let ctx = Z3.mk_context [] in
    let term = Z3.Boolean.mk_true ctx in
    let result = Z3.FloatingPoint.get_ebits ctx (Z3.Expr.get_sort term) in
    Printf.printf "Test Z3: get_ebits: %d.\n" result
```
This will exit with "Segmentation fault".

issue_title: ML API: mk_int: check for overflow
issue_body:
I just discovered a nasty unsoundness https://github.com/verifast/verifast/issues/138 in my tool VeriFast, caused by the fact that `Z3native.mk_int n s`, on platforms where OCaml's `int` type is 63 bits but C's `int` type is 32 bits (which today probably means any platform except Cygwin32; it definitely includes macOS), silently truncates its argument `n`.

The docs for `Z3.mk_int` do (somewhat vaguely) warn about not all integers being supported, but still I would suggest putting a run-time check into `Z3native.mk_int`, something like:
```c
if ((int)Int_val(a1) != Int_val(a1)) { /* throw an OCaml exception */ }
```

Since the Z3native stubs are generated automatically, I guess this should be fixed in `update_api.py`. Furthermore, I guess this issue really applies to each API function that takes an OCaml `int`.

Alternatively, of course, versions of the Z3 C APIs could be implemented and used that take machine words instead of C `int`s.

It seems that MBQI is enabled by default when creating a solver using the Optimize API, and it does not seem possible to disable it. Using the Solver API the option can be set with ```s.set(mbqi=True)``` (for instance), but this raises an exception when using the Optimize API. Is there a reason for this? Is there another way to disable MBQI when using the Optimize API?

To get a better feeing for z3 I wanted to do constraint-based layouting.
https://gist.github.com/daniel-j-h/499c50455929ab6aa755

Surprisingly though, I stumbled upon a strange assertion.
This is triggered only when I set the enclosing box to a resolution of 1366,768 and use the area function (i.e. multiplication). Setting the box to 1365,768 or switching the area constraint with width and height constraints does give me a valid model.

```
ASSERTION VIOLATION
File: ../src/smt/theory_arith_aux.h
Line: 1996
valid_assignment()
(C)ontinue, (A)bort, (S)top, (T)hrow exception, Invoke (G)DB
A
```

I could only trace it back to theory_arith_inv.h's valid_assignment, and then satisfy_integrality. I can see no reason why this assertion could happen. Would you be so kind and have a look at it? Is the multiplication the problem? As far as I read the Ints theory spec, there is an (* Int Int Int :left-assoc) symbol.

This is tested with

    Z3 [version 4.4.0 - 64 bit - build hashcode bd162588b28c1ca38980bfd0df040539df176370].

and also with the latest (as of now)

    Z3 [version 4.4.0 - 64 bit - build hashcode 3ba2e712b21bb5c8e987243c118adbd8b30b185d]

Thanks,
Daniel

issue_title: Presence of lblpos or lblneg changes the outcome of a query
issue_body:
I have been experiencing unpredictable behavior with Z3 version 4.4.1.
I'm attaching four files (very large, sorry). 

Basically, the problem I have is something like this:

A formula like this one is deemed valid:

```
(implies (forall x. (! (implies (p x) (q x) :pattern ((q x)))))
              (q x))
```

But, a formula like this times out (for some uninterpreted boolean `l`).

Case 1:
```
(implies (forall x. (! (implies (or l (p x)) (q x) :pattern ((q x)))))
              (q x))
```

If I try variants like

Case 2:
```
(implies (forall x. (! (implies (! (or l (p x)) :lblpos xx) (q x) :pattern ((q x)))))
              (q x))
```

or
 
Case 3:
```
(implies (forall x. (! (implies (! (or l (p x)) :lblneg xx) (q x) :pattern ((q x)))))
              (q x))
```

Then I start to see behavior that is very hard to predict for some examples. 

The four files below:

[q4-no-lbl-fails.smt2.txt](https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/files/565079/q4-no-lbl-fails.smt2.txt) This is analogous to case 1, above. There are some disjuncts introduced and the query times out.

[q4-ok.smt2.txt](https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/files/565068/q4-ok.smt2.txt) This query should be equivalent to the previous one, except some terms are marked with a lblpos and it succeeds quickly.

[q4-fails-lblpos.smt2.txt](https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/files/565070/q4-fails-lblpos.smt2.txt) This query is identical to the previous one, except more terms are marked lblpos and it times out. 

[q4-ok-lblneg.smt2.txt](https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/files/565071/q4-ok-lblneg.smt2.txt) This one is identical to the previous one, but some lblpos are turned into lblneg and the query succeeds quickly. 

Any hints as to what's going on and how I might avoid this unpredictability would be much appreciated. 

Thanks!

issue_title: Clearing cells sets illegal 'outputs' key to markdown cells
issue_body:
This is a little bit related to #1232  and #1233 

Restarting the kernel and clearing cells sets  `outputs` keys to all cells, including markdown cells, where it is invalid.

I'm on master branch commit 974cdd2b05

## Reproducing

1. create of open any notebook with at least one markdown cell
2. use *Language > Restart and Clear All Cells*
3. save the notebook
4. opening the notebook using `jupyter notebook` or `jupyter nbconvert` will fail in the manner below


I discovered this trying to convert a notebook to markdown:

```
jarmo@lambda-epsilon:/tmp$ jupyter nbconvert --to markdown a.ipynb 
[NbConvertApp] Converting notebook a.ipynb to markdown
[NbConvertApp] ERROR | Notebook JSON is invalid: Additional properties are not allowed ('outputs' was unexpected)

Failed validating 'additionalProperties' in markdown_cell:

On instance['cells'][0]:
{'cell_type': 'markdown',
 'metadata': {'collapsed': False, 'outputExpanded': False},
 'outputs': ['...0 outputs...'],
 'source': 'this cell will have an `outputs` key which is invalid'}
[NbConvertApp] Writing 55 bytes to a.md
```

## Fix

Using a text editor to remove all `outputs` keys form cells that have `"cell_type": "markdown"` fixes the errors.

issue_title: Custom scalars dashboard tries to load data for irrelevant runs
issue_body:
The `tf-line-chart-data-loader` elements used in the scalars plugin via `tf-scalar-card` take a runs list and tag that are specific to that chart, i.e. the tag is the one the chart is displaying and the runs are those that the backend computed have data for this tag, and which are served under the `/tags` endpoint as a JSON object mapping runs to tags to tag metadata:
https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorboard/blob/1.5.0/tensorboard/plugins/scalar/tf_scalar_dashboard/tf-scalar-dashboard.html#L141

This ensures that when the chart loads its data, it issues a `/scalars` request only for run x tag combinations that we expect to actually produce data.

However, the custom scalars plugin always passes the entire list of known runs (i.e. everything the run selector has selected) to its `tf-line-chart-data-loader`-containing elements:
https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorboard/blob/1.5.0/tensorboard/plugins/custom_scalar/tf_custom_scalar_dashboard/tf-custom-scalar-dashboard.html#L174,L189

This results in one `/scalars` request per selected run for each chart on the custom scalars dashboard, regardless of whether there is any data to serve for that tag-regex and run combination.

In a case where there are 100 runs (all of which are selected by default) and only 3 of them are being used for the custom scalars dashboard, this means we send 100 requests instead of 3.  Over a slow network connection this is quite noticeably slow, and causes the custom scalar chart to show the "loader" circle gif for much longer than would otherwise seem needed.  One easy way to repro this behavior is just to run the scalars demo code and the custom scalars demo code, merge the two logdirs, and then load the result (since the scalars demo code creates 18 runs).

I think a more efficient approach would be to have the custom scalars backend basically load from the scalars PluginRunToTagToContent an inverted mapping of tag -> run, then match each custom chart spec's tag regexes/etc. against the tags in that list to get a list of applicable runs, and expose this information to the dashboard in addition to `/layout`.  Then each card would get its own set of applicable runs, and intersect them with the runs selected in the sidebar to produce the runs that should be loaded.  Note that the set of runs per chart would need to be updated on refresh like for the scalars dashboard since it might grow over time.

issue_title: Resizing the debugger plugin does not work at non-default zoom levels.
issue_body:
To repro, navigate to the debugger dashboard in Chrome. Either zoom in or out via Chrome. Resize the left pane. Notice that the mouse will increase in distance (depart) from the resizer as the user drags left or right.

This is because the debugger resizer logic obtains the mouse X position using `event.screenX` during drags. The `screenX` property returns the screen position in device pixels. We should instead use CSS pixels because we resize by updating the CSS width of the left pane.

```
AssertionError: u'0f0e0d0c0b0a09080706050403020100' != '000102030405060708090a0b0c0d0e0f'
```

Test harness requires a way to get commissioning logs, and retrieval some useful information for verification.

For certification test 8_x_x, harness will verify the following messages based on the logs: 
- JOIN_FIN.req
- JOIN_FIN.rsp
- JOIN_ENT.req
- JOIN_ENT.rsp

Since they are all sent in an encrypted DTLS ApplicationData message.

Logs of the upper four messages need to include following information:
- type (0: JOIN_FIN.req, 1: JOIN_FIN.rsp, 2: JOIN_ENT.req, 3: JOIN_ENT.rsp)
- direction (in/out)
- eui
- length
- tlvs payload

While the **type** is used to verify the present of messages, and **tlvs payload** is used to verify the present of tlvs (only verify Provisioning URL TLV for now, not present in test 8_1_1, should present in test 8_1_6).

I noticed that when the Ip6 layer processes a received packet, it first copies the header out of the message to the stack, and then processes the header. Similarly, when sending a packet, it first constructs the header on the stack, and then copies it into the Message. Other layer of the protocol stack (e.g., UDP) work in the same way.

However, if OPENTHREAD_CONFIG_MESSAGE_BUFFER_SIZE is tuned correctly, then all of the headers are likely to be in the first buffer in the Message. The headers won't be split across multiple Message buffers. Therefore, it would be more efficient to just process headers by reading the memory in the Message buffer itself, instead of first copying it to a stack buffer.

This could be accomplished by adding a public method to the Message class:

```
uint8_t* GetPointer(uint16_t aOffset) const;
```

And, of course, one would modify the IP6 and UDP layers to just construct headers (for sending packets) and process headers (for receiving packets) directly in Messages. This would be more efficient than copying messages to the stack.

I would be happy to contribute this fix if the developers are receptive to this idea.

issue_title: using yield in to_array breaks pyexcel/sheets/matrix.py
issue_body:
Hi,

import pyexcel
import pyexcel.ext.xlsx
import pyexcel.utils
workbook = pyexcel.get_book(file_name="sample.xlsx")

breaks, because in Matrix.__init__ -> uniform -> longest_row_number you call "len(array)"
But this array came out of  pyexcel-io's SheetReader.to_array which returns nowadays a generator, so...
TypeError: object of type 'generator' has no len()

IMHO there's no alternative to letting to_array() return a list again, otherwise any "i'm curious how long this thing is"-operations will empty out the generator.
One thing i just tried is:

def uniform(array):
    """Fill-in empty strings to empty cells to make it MxN

    :param list in_array: a list of arrays
    """ 
    # array has rows, a row has cells
    even_array = []
    for row in array:
        tmp_row = []
        for col in row:
            if col is None:
                tmp_row.append("")
            else:
                tmp_row.append(col)
            even_array.append(tmp_row)
    width = max(map(len, even_array))
    # now fill the short rows with empty cells
    for row in even_array:
        row_length = len(row)
        if row_length < width:
            row += [""] * (width - row_length)
    return width, even_array

But i don't know if this is efficient regarding Performance.

Gerhard

    site_latitude = 20

Hi!

As reported in https://github.com/tflearn/tflearn/issues/303 the build_hdf5_image_dataset is VERY slow. I tried to build a hdf5 on 60K images for almost 3 days now! I didn't try using ```image_preloader``` (or at least I closed it after 10-15 minutes while the training didn't start).

1) Is somebody investigating this issue? If not well, I would throw in some hours
2) My "top" suspect is line [408](https://github.com/cristi-zz/tflearn/blob/master/tflearn/data_utils.py#L408) in ```build_hdf5_image_dataset```. The 2'nd suspect is PIL. Why? I am using hdf5 format for a while but always wrote big chunks of data at a time (and the writting goes ~at I/O speed). The file list is built automatically because I have to filter out the "garbage": partially downloaded files, RGBA files, etc. I do this buy opening each file using scikit-image and checking for exceptions, no of channels, etc. Reading 60K images and writting a csv out of them is fast (~minutes) But only the profiling will tell for sure.
3) (somehow unrelated) ```image_preloader``` does the same thing slightly different. Wouldn't it be best that the two methods have the same interface (and of course mostly the same code)?

I have some questions:

1) How hard is to add a new dependency to tflearn? (scikit-image for example)
2) What library do you favour for multithreading? (again, to speed up hdf5 buildup)
3) There are no tests for data_utils.py? 

Thanks!
Cristi

issue_title: poutput() can not print a zero.
issue_body:
Say you built an app which subclasses cmd2. Say you wanted to have an 'exit_code' command in your app which printed out the exit code of the last command processed. Say you wanted to output an exit code of zero, using the poutput() function. It won't print it.

Here's the poutput() function:

https://github.com/python-cmd2/cmd2/blob/5d5675bac5a8d431fbae1e12daf9b27ca4edfe91/cmd2.py#L588-L599

If msg evaluates to false, then nothing is output. An empty string, and a zero both evaluate to false.

Recommended fix:

```
if msg is not None:
```

issue_title: Add synchronization for netting channel external state calls
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

ChannelExternalState methods may be called more than once, e.g.:

- Two concurrent tasks are running against a node that goes offline
- Once each task reaches the unsafe region, both will emit the ContractSendChannelClose
- Without some sort of check, close will be called twice

## Solution

Flag the ChannelExternalState for "waiting_for_close", and only call `close` once.

## Tasklist

- [ ] Write a test
- [ ] Fix the problem

Hello,

While building RNN models, I ran into a totally unexpected issue. 
Given working LSTM (or GRU) code, I had correctly decreasing train loss but increasing val loss.

ALL THINGS BEING EQUAL, changing batch_size from 32 to 4 caused the val loss to move in the correct direction and actually train.  I'm re-running with batch_size = 1 to confirm, but am pretty certain.

This seems potentially related to this "closed" issue.
https://github.com/pytorch/text/issues/70

Is there anything that would cause val/dev to not be properly sorted in larger batches?  My hypothesis is that sentences would then be extremely padded.  e.g. if you put a sentence of length 100 and 5 together, the latter would be padded 95 times.  Another reason might exist due to weird shuffling.  

If this hypothesis is correct, is there a way to fix this (ensure val/dev are sorted the same logical way train is)?  Hopefully a simple flag in BucketIterator or something similar.  
Will answer follow-up ?s or provide code if needed.

```python
from ipyleaflet import Map, Marker, MarkerCluster
m = Map(center=(50, 354), zoom=5) # notice longitude
marker1 = Marker(location=(50, 354)) # notice longitude values
marker2 = Marker(location=(52, 356))
marker3 = Marker(location=(48, 352))
marker_cluster = MarkerCluster(markers=(marker1, marker2, marker3))
m.add_layer(marker_cluster);
m
```

The test for whether a code yielded isn't the inverse of whether it didn't yield, in `trio/testing/_checkpoints.py:_assert_yields_or_not`.

One of these needs to be fixed, but I'm not sufficiently versed in Trio internals to figure out which.

Idea: factor this out to a `yielded` bool, then write the conditional as `if expected and not yielded` … `elif yielded and not expected` clauses.

Workflows in production are often run in the same directory multiple times. Various log files, checkpoint files, submit scripts, config options etc are often produced and ideally stored in a manner that would allow the user to easily identify files associated with a single workflow run. Having a run specific directory would also help #39 and #48. 

I propose that we have run directory that is named runNNN by default and placed in the current directory. Users would have the option to specify a different run directory path, if they so choose.

calls the [deploy_template](https://github.com/localstack/localstack/blob/master/localstack/utils/cloudformation/template_deployer.py#LC484) function regardless whether `action == 'CreateStack' or action == 'UpdateStack'`. So if the action is `'CreateStack'`, the following block

```python
for resource_id, resource in iteritems(next):
            stack_resources = describe_stack_resources(stack_name, resource_id)
            resource['__details__'] = stack_resources[0]
```

in the `deploy_template` function attempts to `describe_stack_resources` of a nonexistent stack which appears to be the cause of the error.

To reproduce:

```bash
$ sed -i 's:v3.6:edge:g' /etc/apk/repositories
$ apk update
$ apk add python py-pip

    url = self._construct_url(id_=obj_id, obj=obj, parameters=params)
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/gitlab/__init__.py", line 261, in _construct_url
```

As an experimenter
I want to have experiments creating queues to be able to specify dead letter queues
In order that poison pill, or continually failing messages do not overwhelm runners

http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSSimpleQueueService/latest/SQSDeveloperGuide/sqs-dead-letter-queues.html

PubSub does not appear to have explicit support for this any strategy for grooming out these messages implies a need for a central record of message attempts and this is not good. We will have to take on some design work to determine what to do in the Google case.

Is there a possible bug in Conv operator defined in ConvOpSchemaGenerator?
```
schema.NumInputs(2, 3);
            schema.NumOutputs(1);
            schema.Input(0,
                         "X",
                         "Input data tensor from previous layer; has size (N x C x H x W)"
                         ", where N is the batch size, C is the number of channels, and"
                         " H and W are the height and width. Note that this is for the 2D image."
                         "Otherwise the size is (N x D1 x D2 ... x Dn)");
            schema.Input(1,
                         "weights",
                         "The weight tensor that will be used in the convolutions; "
                         "has size (M x C x kH x kW), where C is the number of channels, "
                         "and kH and kW are the height and width of the kernel, and M is the number "
                         "of feature maps. For more than 2 dimensions, the kernel shape will be "
                         "(M x C x k1 x k2 x ... x kn), where is the dimension of the kernel");
            schema.Output(0,
                          "Y",
                          "Output data tensor that contains the result of the convolution. The "
                          "output dimensions are functions of the kernel size, stride size, "
                          "and pad lengths.");
```
Input 2 as bias missing here?

BTW, there's no 'num_output' attribute for conv, conv_transpose, fullyconnected, and no 'use_bias' attribute as well. 
In other words, these attributes are not retrievable in graph, instead, we have to parse these attributes from the initializers, this design is obviously not friendly to frameworks other than caffe2.

Am I missing something?
